title: Extend enumerate_commits.py with explicit-list mode for parallel-workstream closures

description:
Implement deploy support for Section 12.5 (Role-Aware Visibility) and Section 12.7 (Field-Level Permissions) on EspoCRM 9.x. v1.2 left both sections NOT_SUPPORTED per DEC-243 (12.5) and prior deferrals (12.7). The right implementation path requires real wire-format research; current best candidates are documented in DEC-243 and the schema §12.5 Deploy Support sub-section. 

Scope items to investigate at PI-051 kickoff: (1) Dynamic Handler JavaScript generation — `client/custom/src/views/<entity>/record/detail.js` generation per entity with role-aware visibility logic; requires file-system writes (no REST API path) and an audit-side reverse-engineering strategy for round-trip; or accept that this surface is NOT_AUDITABLE and stays write-only. (2) Teams-as-proxies-for-Roles translation — auto-generate a Team per Role at deploy; bind users to teams as a separate operator step; use EspoCRM 8.1's current-user-teams Dynamic Logic condition. Requires conflating Team and Role semantics or accepting that operators see auto-generated teams in the EspoCRM admin UI. (3) Upstream EspoCRM feature request — file a Dynamic Logic role-condition feature request and wait. Not actionable as a planning item but worth tracking. (4) Layout Sets + Teams (forRoles variant deploy path) — separate from 12.5.1 deploy; layout-level role scoping can use Layout Sets bound to auto-generated mirror-Teams even if leaf-clause role conditions remain NOT_SUPPORTED.

For §12.7 (field-level permissions): out of scope for v1.2 because the schema sub-section was deferred from v1.1. v1.4 should consider §12.5 and §12.7 holistically since both are role-keyed permission surfaces with similar deploy mechanics on EspoCRM (probably some combination of role-record fieldOverridesPermission columns and dynamic-logic for visibility).

Predecessors: audit-v1.2 series complete (this close-out). Schema spec §12.5 and §12.6 patched at v1.2; §12.7 sub-section still in draft state from prior work. Successor: actual v1.4 schema-spec revisions plus implementation prompts. No active commitment to a target date — PI-051 enters the backlog at status Open.
executive_summary: Implement deploy support for Section 12.5 (Role-Aware Visibility) and Section 12.7 (Field-Level Permissions) on EspoCRM 9.x. v1.2 left both sections NOT_SUPPORTED per DEC-243 (12.5) and prior deferrals (12.7). The right implementation path requires real wire-format research; current best candidates are documented in DEC-243 and the schema §12.5 Deploy Support sub-section. (2) Teams-as-proxies-for-Roles translation — auto-generate a Team per Role at deploy; bind users to teams as a separate operator step; use EspoCRM 8.1's current-user-teams Dynamic Logic condition.
title: audit-v1.4 — Section 12.5 role-aware visibility deploy implementation alongside Section 12.7 field-level permissions

description: Per DEC-245's architectural pivot away from MCP-via-claude.ai (blocked by upstream Anthropic bug per DEC-244). Scope: build a chat UI that gives Doug Claude conversations with access to the v2 governance database tool surface. Architecture: Python client using the Anthropic SDK with native tools=[...] definitions, calling the REST API at 127.0.0.1:8765 directly. Reuse `crmbuilder_v2.mcp_server.tools` module functions (already wrap httpx → REST API) as the native tool definitions — no rewrite required, their signatures and docstrings can be auto-converted to Anthropic API tool schemas. Loop: read user input → messages.create(tools=...) → execute any tool_use blocks via httpx → feed tool_result back → repeat until Claude responds without further tool calls. Phase 1 (proof of concept, ~30 min): a terminal-based ~50 LOC Python script that demonstrates the loop end-to-end with a handful of tools. Phase 2 (production surface): pick one of (a) terminal-based UI with Rich/Textual, (b) PySide6 chat tab inside the existing CRMBuilder desktop app, (c) Streamlit/Gradio web prototype, (d) custom FastAPI + React/HTMX web app on the existing Cloudflare Tunnel — choice deferred until Phase 1 completes. No MCP HTTP transport involved. MCP server in stdio mode for Claude Desktop continues to work unaffected. Cloudflare Tunnel + Access + Managed OAuth infrastructure preserved on disk (Path B work in commit fc4690c) for future use if/when Anthropic fixes the connector bug.

description:
**Explain it like I was 5:** Every time the project writes something to its notebook, the project also makes a photocopy of the page so people without a notebook can read it. The notebook recently learned how to track code-change records (CM-NNNN rows), but nobody told the photocopier — so those new pages never get photocopied. People without the notebook can't see them.

Discovered during the SES-078 (PI-002) close-out apply on 2026-05-25: CM-0005 was created in the v2 DB as part of the close-out, but `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/commits.json` was not regenerated and does not exist on disk. Root cause: `crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/exporter.py` defines `_EXPORT_TABLES` (the ordered list of `(filename, model)` pairs the snapshot builder iterates) and that list does not include the `Commit` model. Every other v0.7 governance entity (workstream, conversation, reference_book, reference_book_versions, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) is in the list; Commit was missed when the model landed in v0.7. Engagement is correctly handled by the separate `meta_exporter.py` writing to `db-export/meta/engagements.json` — not affected.

Impact: file-fallback Tier 2 readers (Claude sessions without MCP connectivity per CLAUDE.md's session orientation protocol) cannot see commit records. CM-0001..CM-0005 (existing commit records, including PI-002's build commit and PI-030's slice A/B/C commits) are queryable only via the live REST API. PI-031's commits panel UI reads from the API and is not affected; PI-033's historical backfill writes via the API and is not affected.

Fix scope (single-line patch + test + backfill):
  1. Add `("commits", Commit)` to `_EXPORT_TABLES` in `crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/exporter.py`, alongside the v0.7 governance entities (between `deposit_events` and `references` is the natural position).
  2. Add `Commit` to the model imports if not already present.
  3. Extend `tests/crmbuilder_v2/access/test_exporter.py` to assert `commits.json` is written when a Commit row exists.
  4. One-shot backfill: trigger a write that regenerates the snapshot to produce `commits.json` containing CM-0001..CM-0005 (and any commits added between this PI's creation and its build) and commit it.

Related observation surfaced alongside this PI: the `crmbuilder-v2-api` server process is long-running and does not hot-reload on code changes; operators must restart the API for newly-merged changes to take effect. Surfaced during PI-002's close-out when the next planning_item POST (this PI) was attempted via the new optional-identifier path and the still-running pre-PI-002 server rejected with `body.identifier — Field required`. Not blocking; not separately filed (known property of any long-running uvicorn process; no autoreload in production deployments by design).

This PI belongs to WS-009 (Code Change Lifecycle workstream) — natural home alongside PI-031/PI-032/PI-033.

The notebook recently learned how to track code-change records (CM-NNNN rows), but nobody told the photocopier — so those new pages never get photocopied. People without the notebook can't see them. Every other v0.7 governance entity (workstream, conversation, reference_book, reference_book_versions, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) is in the list; Commit was missed when the model landed in v0.7.
title: Add Commit model to access/exporter.py _EXPORT_TABLES so commits.json regenerates with the rest of the engagement snapshot

description:
Starting state: V2's storage system is engagement-partitioned. Each engagement has its own database holding governance records (decisions, sessions, planning items, references, work tickets) and methodology records (entities, processes, personas). There is no cross-engagement or global store for shared reference knowledge.

Problem: Several emerging content types are cross-engagement by nature:
- Glossary terms (TERM-NNN) — same definitions apply across all engagements
- Skills (domain-specific knowledge files loaded contextually based on client defining statements)
- Patterns (industry-typical structures, e.g., nonprofit mentoring, charitable foundation)
- Inventories (reference inventories of typical entities/personas for common domain types)
- Possibly: authoring standards, identifier conventions, rendering templates

These belong in V2 per the 'database is single source of truth' principle, but don't fit the engagement-partitioned model as it exists.

Target state: an architectural decision and implementation for where cross-engagement reference content lives in V2. Options to evaluate when this PI is worked:
- A new global/shared V2 store alongside per-engagement databases
- A reserved 'master' engagement holding shared content; others reference it
- An extension to the existing model that supports cross-engagement reads
- A separate reference data tier with its own schema and access patterns

Scope when worked:
- Architectural decision recorded selecting one of the above approaches
- V2 schema changes to implement the selected approach
- API endpoints, MCP tool surface, desktop UI for the new store
- Migration of existing MD-scaffolded content (glossary, future skills) into the new store
- Established pattern for adding new cross-engagement reference content

Dependencies:
- Once resolved, supersedes the temporary MD scaffolding approach for glossary, skills, and similar Category 3 content
- PI-061 (glossary V2 migration) is dependent on this PI

Why deferred: immediate needs are met by MD scaffolds. The architectural decision is significant and shouldn't be made under time pressure.

description:
CURRENT STATE (to be replaced):

V2 currently has two governance entity types in a 1:0..1 relationship:
- conversation (introduced by Decision 119, V0.7 governance entity release): represents the lifecycle of a single Claude.ai chat through states planned -> kickoff_drafted -> ready -> in_flight -> complete (or cancelled, superseded). One Claude.ai chat = one conversation record.
- session: an append-only completion record written when a conversation closes. Captures identifier, title, topics_covered, artifacts_produced, conversation_reference.

These names collide with everyday English (Claude.ai's 'conversation' is a chat window; 'session' is too generic), and the 1:0..1 relationship is a strong indicator that they're really one concept split for historical reasons.

TARGET STATE:

A redesigned two-entity model where the entities are NOT 1:0..1 but 1:N, and the names match everyday English:
- session (redesigned): a discrete unit of communication in any medium. One Claude.ai chat = one session. One email = one session. One phone call = one session. One Zoom meeting = one session. One in-person meeting = one session. Medium-agnostic with a medium-type classifier and medium-specific metadata (subject + thread ID for email; URL for chat; dial-in for phone; location for in-person).
- conversation (redesigned): a focused topical discussion that takes place WITHIN a session. One session contains one or more conversations. Each conversation has its own lifecycle (planned, started, concluded, not-started).

Conversations are session-scoped — they do not span sessions. Cross-session topical continuity is expressed via explicit reference edges:
- conversation_follows_from: a new conversation in a new session continues a topic from a prior conversation in a prior session.
- conversation_relates_to: a conversation is relevant to another conversation without being a direct successor (related topics, prior context).

Each conversation links to its outputs (decisions, planning items, references, entity work, etc.) via the universal references table.

OPEN DESIGN QUESTIONS to resolve when worked:
1. Session-level sequencing. In an email thread, individual emails are clearly in sequence. Should sessions ALSO carry predecessor/successor links (session_follows_from), or is session sequencing implicit through the conversation follows_from chains within them?
2. Handling of planned-but-not-started conversations at session close. When a session planned three topical conversations but only got to two, the third has not been started. Two candidate handlings: (a) The unstarted conversation record exists in not_started state and gets either rescheduled or marked deferred. (b) The unstarted conversation never gets a conversation record; it stays as a Planning Item that becomes a conversation when a future session picks it up.
3. Medium-specific metadata schema. What fields are universal vs medium-specific? Candidate: shared base fields plus a JSON or sub-record for medium-specific data.
4. Migration of existing V2 records. Current V2 conversation records become sessions under the new model; current V2 session records become session completion data. Topical breakdown within existing chats would need to be either reconstructed or accepted as single-conversation sessions for historical records.

SCOPE WHEN WORKED:
- Architectural decision recording the redesigned model.
- Schema spec updates for session and conversation.
- V2 schema migration: rename existing entities, add new fields (medium, sequencing links), add new entity if a new sub-unit table is needed.
- API endpoint updates per V2 envelope pattern.
- MCP tool surface updates.
- Desktop UI updates (conversation panel as sub-unit within session view).
- Migration of existing records to the new shape.
- Documentation updates throughout (CLAUDE.md, working conventions, PRD references to the old model).

Why deferred: substantive architectural change with downstream impact on V2 schema, code, and existing records. Captured here so it gets proper attention; immediate work continues using current V2 with the understanding that this PI is in queue.
executive_summary: CURRENT STATE (to be replaced): V2 currently has two governance entity types in a 1:0..1 relationship: - conversation (introduced by Decision 119, V0.7 governance entity release): represents the lifecycle of a single Claude.ai chat through states planned -> kickoff_drafted -> ready -> in_flight -> complete (or cancelled, superseded). One Claude.ai chat = one conversation record. - session: an append-only completion record written when a conversation closes.
title: Redesign Session and Conversation entities — medium-agnostic communication container with topical sub-units

description:
Audience-facing summary: Adds a required 'executive summary' field to every planning item, decision, and session record. Today these records carry only implementer-facing description text, so a product manager or executive reading the governance log has to parse engineering detail to extract the gist. With this field in place, governance records become legible to non-technical reviewers without losing the technical content for implementers.

Technical scope: Add 'executive_summary' column to the planning_items, decisions, and sessions tables via a single Alembic migration. Type TEXT NOT NULL with a CHECK constraint enforcing length between 200 and 800 characters. Update the access-layer validators in planning_items.py, decisions.py, and sessions.py to require the field on create and update. Update the Pydantic models in the API to include the field. Update the snapshot regenerator to include executive_summary in db-export JSON output for all three tables. The migration runs against existing rows — see PI-075 for the backfill of populated values; the NOT NULL constraint must be deferred to the post-backfill migration in PI-075, or the column added as nullable here and made NOT NULL in PI-075.

description:
Audience-facing summary: Writes an audience-facing summary on every existing governance record so the new field is populated retroactively. Without this, the new executive_summary field would be empty on every record created before PI-074 landed, defeating the purpose of having it.

Technical scope: AI-assisted backfill pass over all ~51 existing planning_items, ~243 existing decisions, and ~76 existing sessions in the CRMBUILDER engagement. For each record, generate a 200-800 character PM/exec-readable summary by reading the existing description/context/decision/rationale/summary fields and rewriting them for a non-technical audience. Spot-check at minimum 10 records per type before bulk-applying. Submit summaries via API PATCH (or directly via the access layer) one record at a time so the change_log captures the audit trail. Finalize the NOT NULL constraint on the column (if PI-074 deferred it) after all rows are populated.

description:
Audience-facing summary: Lets each planning item declare which areas of the codebase or methodology it touches. The orchestrator uses these area labels to figure out which planning items are safe to run in parallel without two agents editing the same files at the same time. Without this field, parallel dispatch is unsafe.

Technical scope: Add 'area' column to the planning_items table via Alembic migration. Type JSON NOT NULL with a CHECK constraint enforcing json_array_length >= 1 and every element in the registered vocabulary. Register a new vocabulary 'area' in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py with the initial seventeen values listed in orchestrator-planning.md §2.1 (v2-storage, v2-access, v2-api, v2-mcp, v2-ui, cbm-mn, cbm-mr, cbm-cr, cbm-fu, cbm-services, methodology-interviews, methodology-process, methodology-templates, methodology-product, infrastructure, v1-automation, v1-espo, v1-programs). Update the planning_items access-layer validator to enforce the vocabulary on create and update. Update the API Pydantic model. Update the snapshot regenerator. NOT NULL constraint is deferred until PI-083 backfills existing rows; add the column as JSON nullable here, tighten to NOT NULL in PI-083.

description:
Audience-facing summary: Lets the orchestrator mark a planning item as 'currently being worked on by a specific agent' so two agents never grab the same item at once. The fields are null when a planning item is available, populated when an agent claims it, and cleared when the agent's work resolves or is rolled back.

Technical scope: Add 'claimed_by' (nullable foreign-key-style string column referencing either a conversation_identifier or a session_identifier — to be decided in this PI) and 'claimed_at' (nullable timestamp) columns to the planning_items table via Alembic migration. Update the access-layer validators to enforce 'both fields set or both null'. Decide whether claimed_by references the conversation entity (semantically the conversation holds the claim) or the session entity (simpler to write at start-of-work). Update API Pydantic model and snapshot regenerator. Add a small access-layer helper claim_planning_item(identifier, claimant) that sets the pair atomically with optimistic concurrency (fail if already claimed). No backfill needed — existing rows stay null.

description:
Audience-facing summary: Lets the orchestrator atomically reserve a block of identifiers (for example, the next five session IDs) at the start of a run, so child agents don't race to claim the next available number when they finish their work. Small API addition with outsized impact on orchestrator safety — without it, two child agents finishing close in time could collide on the next-SES identifier.

Technical scope: Add 'POST /identifiers/reserve' endpoint accepting {entity_type, count} and returning {reserved: [list of identifiers], head_after}. Atomic advancement of the engagement-scoped identifier head, with the reserved block held server-side under the requesting conversation's claim. A subsequent POST of a record with an identifier in the reserved block consumes the reservation. Stale reservations (no record posted within a configurable TTL, default 1 hour) auto-release. Update API documentation. Add access-layer tests. Supports the standard prefixed identifier types (SES, DEC, PI, CONV, RISK, TOPIC, CHA, STAT, and any other client-computed identifier kinds).

title: Implement identifier reservation API endpoint

description:
Audience-facing summary: The single API call the orchestrator makes at the start of every run to figure out what's safe to dispatch. Returns the open planning items grouped by dependency depth (planning items at depth 0 have no unresolved dependencies; depth 1 depends on depth 0; etc.) with each item's area set attached. Without this, the orchestrator would have to make many separate calls and run the topological sort itself.

Technical scope: Add 'GET /orchestration/ready-batches' endpoint scoped to the active engagement. Returns {batches: [{depth: 0, items: [{identifier, title, executive_summary, area, claimed_by}, ...]}, {depth: 1, items: [...]}, ...]}. Computes the dependency graph by reading blocked_by references where source_type=planning_item and target_type=planning_item and source.status=Open. Resolved items are filtered out. Already-claimed items appear in their batch but with claimed_by populated so the orchestrator can skip them. Topological sort handles cycles by surfacing them as a separate 'cyclic' bucket with a warning rather than failing. Optional query parameters: area (filter to just specific areas), max_depth (cut off the sort early).

description:
Audience-facing summary: Adds the relationship type that connects the orchestrator's supervising conversation to each of its child agents' conversations. Without it, the governance timeline can't show the parent-child structure of a parallel run, and audit queries can't walk from an orchestrator session to its children.

Technical scope: Add 'conversation_orchestrates_conversation' to REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py. Extend the _kinds_for_pair logic to allow the (conversation, conversation) source/target pair with this new kind. Alembic migration to extend the refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint to include the new kind. Update the V2 desktop UI's references-create dialog to expose the new option in the cascading filter (this is automatic from the vocab/rules update — no UI code changes needed; flagged here as a verification step). Per CLAUDE.md, adding a relationship kind requires updating REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS, _kinds_for_pair, and the CHECK constraint together.

description:
Audience-facing summary: The orchestrator itself — a Python script that runs at the developer's terminal, reads the open planning-item backlog via the ready-batches API, plans the wave structure, spawns parallel child agents (one per area-disjoint cluster), monitors them, and produces its own close-out payload referencing each child's close-out. The largest deliverable in this workstream and the proof that the rest of the foundation works.

Technical scope: Python script under crmbuilder-v2/scripts/orchestrator/run.py. Pre-flight: clean git status, current engagement set, API health check, no other orchestrator currently running (file lock under ~/.crmbuilder-v2/). Main loop: GET /orchestration/ready-batches → reserve identifiers via POST /identifiers/reserve (one SES + one CONV per child + one for the orchestrator itself) → POST workstream-membership and records_session edges for the orchestrator's own conversation → for each area-disjoint cluster in the current wave, create a child branch, write a kickoff file from the template (PI-082) with the agent's assigned identifiers and PI list embedded, spawn a Claude Code subagent against the branch → poll until all agents in the wave complete or fail → on completion, ingest each child's close-out payload reference and create the conversation_orchestrates_conversation edge → advance to next wave. On any failure: halt, log the failure context, leave claimed_by populated for forensic review. Produces a final close-out payload referencing every child's CONV via conversation_orchestrates_conversation.

The largest deliverable in this workstream and the proof that the rest of the foundation works. Technical scope: Python script under crmbuilder-v2/scripts/orchestrator/run.py.
title: Build orchestrator driver script

description:
Audience-facing summary: The standardized kickoff prompt that each child agent receives when the orchestrator dispatches it. Tells the agent which planning items it owns, which branch to work on, which session/conversation identifiers it was pre-allocated, which areas it's claimed, and how to produce its close-out payload at the end. Without this template, every orchestrator run would invent kickoff prompts ad hoc and the agents would have inconsistent behavior.

Technical scope: Template file at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/orchestrator/child-agent-kickoff-template.md with placeholder substitution markers ({{session_identifier}}, {{conversation_identifier}}, {{planning_items}}, {{areas_claimed}}, {{branch_name}}, etc.). Body sections: operating mode (DETAIL by default for code work), engagement and workstream context, pre-allocated identifiers, planning items in scope (full descriptions from the ready-batches response), areas claimed (and the conflict rule explaining the agent must not touch other areas), close-out payload requirements (the agent produces its own ses_NNN.json and apply prompt at the engagement's close-out-payloads directory), branch and commit conventions (branch name, commit message prefix). The template is rendered by the orchestrator driver (PI-081) at dispatch time and written into the working directory of the spawned Claude Code subagent.

title: Build child agent kickoff prompt template

description:
Audience-facing summary: Assigns area labels to every currently-open planning item so the orchestrator can categorize them when it dispatches. Without this backfill, open items have null areas and the orchestrator can't include them in any batch.

Technical scope: AI-assisted pass over the ~33 currently-open planning items in the CRMBUILDER engagement (count as of SES-079; rerun against current open list at PI execution time). For each open PI, read its title and description, infer the affected area(s) from the language (file paths, subsystem names, methodology vs. product framing, etc.), and propose a multi-valued area set. Spot-check at least the first 10 with Doug before bulk-applying. PATCH each PI via API. After backfill, tighten the area column's NOT NULL constraint (deferred from PI-076). Closed/Resolved PIs do not need backfill — area is meaningful only for open work the orchestrator might dispatch. The backfill is one-shot; future PIs declare area at authoring time.

description:
Produce a canonical rules document that codifies the protocols every session and conversation operating against any V2-tracked engagement must follow. The document is Category 3 reference content per DEC-296 (Process Support Knowledge/Tools). Authored as v0.1 DISCUSSION DRAFT, iterated through discussion, and promoted to v1.0 once stable. The rules apply to AI and human agents equally.

Proposed section structure (from the conversation that produced this PI):

1. Purpose and scope — what the document is; who it binds (every conversation against any V2-tracked engagement); relationship to DEC-300 (identifier-capture) and DEC-310 (rules document required).

2. The conversation lifecycle rules — what a conversation MUST do at start, during, and at end. Pre-flight identifier-head capture. Reading the rules document. Acknowledging it. Real-time governance authoring throughout. Close-out per the completeness rules at end.

3. What gets recorded — explicit enumeration of governance content types and their triggers. Decision reached -> DEC. Work identified -> PI. Relationship named -> REF. Artifact produced -> commit row plus references. Deferral -> PI plus rationale.

4. What does NOT count as recording — explicit list of failure modes. Work named only in a DEC's `consequences`. Work named only in `in_flight_at_end`. Work named only in chat prose. Work named in a future 'we should also' without immediately surfacing a PI. Decisions reached but not recorded as DECs.

5. The decision-disposition pairing rule — when a DEC affects an existing artifact, the conversation MUST also author the disposition decision (replace, rename, restructure, demote, retire) AND the PI to do the work, AND the `addresses` reference. No exceptions, no 'obvious mechanical follow-on' rationalizations.

6. Close-out completeness audit — explicit checklist the close-out author runs before sealing the payload. For each DEC: 'does its consequences name work? if so, is the work a PI?' For each PI: 'does it reference its originating DEC?' For each commit: 'is it in commits[]?' For each placeholder phrase ('we should also,' 'this needs,' 'follow-up:') anywhere in the payload: 'is there a PI?'

7. The rules-following protocol — how every conversation discovers and applies these rules. Where the document lives. How CLAUDE.md surfaces it. How the kickoff prompt template references it. How the conversation acknowledges it has read them. What to do when a rule is violated mid-conversation (record the violation as a governance event; do not paper over it).

8. Enforcement and audit — the mechanisms that make following the rules verifiable, not optional. Automated payload validators that fail on unaddressed `consequences` work. Pre-commit hooks on close-out payloads. Periodic database audits that flag pre-rules DECs missing their PIs and produce retroactive-fix PIs.

9. Scope of jurisdiction — applies to the CRMBUILDER engagement and to any other engagement operating under this methodology; this is a methodology rule, not a CRMBuilder-specific rule.

10. Open questions and known limitations — items the v0.1 draft doesn't resolve and that subsequent versions will address.

Drafting order: 1 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6 -> 2 -> 3 -> 7 -> 8 -> 9 -> 10. Sections 3-6 are where the discipline lives; lifecycle, distribution, and enforcement sections matter only once the substance is solid.

Why deferred: this PI's work is the rules document itself; the immediate governance acts were DEC-310 and this PI. Pending: explicit go-ahead to begin drafting, and decision on whether to draft within a continuation of the current Claude.ai conversation or as the focus of the next dedicated session.

The rules apply to AI and human agents equally.

description:
Author the Process PRD for the Session/Conversation governance Process — the lifecycle of a Claude.ai conversation (or other communication unit) from start through close-out, including all governance recording rules. This PRD is where DEC-310's rules-required mandate is fulfilled: the rules are encoded as the Process's steps, anti-patterns, and acceptance criteria, not as a separate document.

Process PRD content includes:
- Process metadata: code, name, domain (from PI-085), owner Persona, version
- Trigger: a Claude.ai conversation begins against a V2-tracked engagement
- Inputs: engagement context, working artifact state, prior session records as relevant
- Process steps (numbered, sequential, with conditions and persona attributions):
  1. Read CLAUDE.md and confirm engagement
  2. Capture pre-flight identifier heads from live V2 API (per DEC-300)
  3. Acknowledge governance recording rules (this Process PRD itself)
  4. Begin substantive work
  5. At each decision-point: author DEC + paired PI + addressing reference at moment-of-decision (no batching)
  6. At each work-surfacing: author PI + addressing reference at moment-of-surfacing
  7. At each disposition affecting an existing artifact: author the disposition DEC + revision PI explicitly
  8. At each artifact production: capture commit row with the full ten-field schema
  9. Re-check identifier heads before any mid-conversation amendment that touches identifier slots
  10. Run close-out completeness audit before sealing the payload
  11. Author close-out payload + apply prompt
  12. Commit and push (per push convention — sandbox commits-and-pushes, Claude Code commits without pushing)
- Entities touched (create / update / read for each):
  - Create: Session, Conversation, Decision, Planning Item, Reference, Commit row, Close-Out Payload (lazy), Deposit Event (lazy)
  - Update: Planning Item (via resolves), existing references where supersession applies
  - Read: existing decisions, planning items, references, work tickets for context
- Personas involved (from PI-086): AI Agent (sandbox or Claude Code), Engagement Lead, others as relevant
- Exception handling: identifier collision (re-query heads, renumber, possibly use --ours merge resolution); apply failure (read deposit_event for diagnostic, patch payload, re-apply); rule violation mid-conversation (record violation as a governance event, do not paper over)
- Acceptance criteria: close-out completeness audit checklist — for each DEC, does its consequences name work AND is the work a PI? For each PI, does it reference an originating DEC? For each commit, is it in commits[] with the full ten-field schema? For each placeholder phrase ('we should also,' 'this needs,' 'follow-up:'), is there a PI?
- Outputs: applied governance records in V2; working artifacts (PRDs, code, configurations) committed to git; close-out payload + apply prompt committed; deposit_event logged

Use bootstrapped methodology since the formal Process PRD Definition Process (PI-088) doesn't yet exist. Observing the practice used to produce this PRD becomes the input to PI-088.

File path: TBD. Candidate: `specifications/{domain-code}/{process-code}.md`.

Output: v0.1 DISCUSSION DRAFT Process PRD that fulfills DEC-310's rules-required mandate by encoding the rules as the Process's structure.

Blocked by PI-085 (needs the Domain) and PI-086 (needs the Personas).

description:

- Process steps: how to gather context, how to identify trigger / steps / entities / personas / exception handling / acceptance criteria, how to author the draft, review cycles, finalization
- Entities touched: Process (create), Domain (read), Persona (read), Entity (read for references)
- Personas involved: AI Agent, Engagement Lead, possibly Domain Owner
- Exception handling: incomplete upstream Domain/Personas (proceed with bootstrapped methodology and refine), conflicting Process descriptions, etc.

- Outputs: a complete Process PRD ready for use

Once authored, this meta Process PRD becomes the standard for all future Process PRD authoring in CRMBuilder and any engagement operating under this methodology.

File path: TBD. Candidate: `specifications/{methodology-domain-code}/process-prd-definition.md`.

description:
Implement Cross Domain Service as a first-class V2 entity so methodology artifacts produced in Phase 6 of the document production process (Notes, Email, Calendar, Surveys, etc.) become queryable, cross-referenceable, and governable alongside Domain, Persona, Process, and Entity.

Scope:
- Alembic migration adding the cross_domain_service table (identifier prefix CDS-NNN, plus core fields: name, description, status, owner, version, file_path)
- Access-layer module under crmbuilder_v2/access/ with CRUD plus next-identifier helper and SAVEPOINT-retry per PI-002
- REST endpoints under crmbuilder_v2/api/ following the {data, meta, errors} envelope; POST accepts null/omitted identifier per PI-002
- Desktop panel under the appropriate sidebar group with the standard EntityCrudDialog pattern
- Reference relationship vocabulary updates in crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py — at minimum: cross_domain_service used_by domain, process invokes cross_domain_service, entity touched_by cross_domain_service; plus the CHECK-constraint migration on refs.relationship_kind
- db-export emitter for the new table
- Tests covering access layer, API, and edge rules

Open design questions deferred to PI execution:
- Should Cross Domain Services be scoped per Engagement, or shared across Engagements as global methodology assets?
- Does the entity need version history (like reference_book has reference_book_versions)?
- How does it interact with Domain — explicit reference edge, or implicit through which Domains invoke it?

Addresses DEC-313.

description:
Author a Python validator that runs in apply_close_out.py pre-flight, checking every close-out payload against the rules in specifications/governance-recording-rules.md before any record is POSTed to the V2 API. The validator becomes the executable form of the rules document: any rule the document states must either map to an existing schema/access-layer constraint OR to a check in this validator. Catches violations before they hit the DB; fast feedback with clear error messages.

Scope of v0.1 checks (drawn from the rules document):
(1) Decision status enum — reject any DEC with status not in {Active, Deleted, Superseded, Withdrawn}. DB constraint already rejects; validator catches earlier with actionable error and prevents partial applies.
(2) Decision back-reference — every DEC in the payload has a decided_in reference back to the session's SES in the references array.
(3) PI item_type — for CRMBUILDER target, every PI in the payload carries item_type 'pending_work'.
(4) WT file_path — every work_ticket carries work_ticket_file_path regardless of work_ticket_kind, including 'other'.
(5) Conversation parentage — every CONV record (or implied by the session) has a conversation_belongs_to_workstream reference to a valid WS-NNN present in the live API, plus the conversation_records_session reference back to its session.
(6) Reference field key — references use the API-style key 'relationship' (not the DB-column-style 'relationship_kind'). Catches the SES-051/052 mismatch class.
(7) Required payload sections — payload contains label, session, conversation, work_tickets, planning_items, commits, decisions, references, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items. Empty arrays acceptable; missing keys are not.
(8) Identifier-head conflicts — every identifier in the payload exceeds the live API's current head for its record type at validation time.
(9) Session date format — new sessions use YYYY-MM-DD (warning, not reject; field is non-normative on apply).
(10) Decision status 'Final' — explicit reject with reference to SES-067, the highest-frequency mistake class.

Non-goals for v0.1: full schema validation (access layer already does that), workstream authoring rules (workstreams aren't in close-out payloads under current convention), MCP-side validation (separate workstream).

Expected output: a validator module invoked from apply_close_out.py pre-flight; exit code non-zero on validation failure with structured error report listing each violation. Validator's rule list is documented inline so the rules document and the validator stay synchronized — a rule added to the document without a corresponding validator check is a known gap.

Workstream assignment: candidate for a new workstream (provisionally WS-013, 'Governance recording enforcement') if and when the related work (MCP-side validation, audit reports) gets prioritized; otherwise can slot into an existing workstream at authoring time.

Delivers: enforcement layer #4 from the SES-096 conversation, supporting the broader strategy of layered enforcement for governance recording rules (DEC-310).
executive_summary: Author a Python validator that runs in apply_close_out.py pre-flight, checking every close-out payload against the rules in specifications/governance-recording-rules.md before any record is POSTed to the V2 API. The validator becomes the executable form of the rules document: any rule the document states must either map to an existing schema/access-layer constraint OR to a check in this validator. Catches violations before they hit the DB; fast feedback with clear error messages.
title: Build apply-pipeline validator that checks close-out payloads against governance-recording-rules.md

description:
Per DEC-319, SES-098's Phase 2 candidate inventory was captured as a durable Markdown document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/CRMBuilder-Phase-2-Candidate-Inventory.md rather than written directly to V2 records, because the v0.8 close-out payload schema is governance-only (session/conversation/work_tickets/planning_items/commits/decisions/references/resolves_planning_items/addresses_planning_items) — no methodology section.

Doug's explicit requirement at confirmation: 'convert MD document to database record when the closeout allows.' This PI tracks that conversion.

When the V2 close-out pipeline supports methodology candidate ingestion (whether via a v0.9 payload schema extension, a separate methodology-write apply path, an MCP tool surface for batch ingestion, or another mechanism), use the MD inventory as the source-of-truth to write to V2:
- 14 Domain records at status: candidate (full list and descriptions in §1 of the MD)
- 9 Persona records at status: candidate (full list and Rule 2.2 results in §3 of the MD); backings TBD pending PI-091
- ~40 Entity records at status: candidate (per-domain lists in §2 of the MD), with appropriate source attribution (V1, V2, or spec)

Plus the references that link them:
- domain-contains-entity for each entity-to-domain relationship
- persona-backed-by-entity or persona-backed-external per Rule 2.2 results
- persona-participates-in-domain for each persona-to-primary-domain relationship

Includes a design step (sub-task): determine the ingestion mechanism. Options:
(a) v0.9 payload schema extension adding methodology arrays (domains, personas, entities) to the close-out — most aligned with existing close-out discipline; requires schema work plus apply_close_out.py extension.
(b) Separate methodology-write apply path — own script, own validation, own ordering rules; less invasive to existing close-out infrastructure.
(c) MCP tool surface for batch methodology ingestion — useful for live-session ingestion patterns; less useful for backfill of MD-captured inventories.
(d) Inline curl POSTs in apply prompts — least disciplined; mentioned for completeness only.

The mechanism choice is itself a design decision worth its own session and should be captured as a DEC at that session's close-out.

The MD inventory is the source-of-truth for CRMBuilder's Phase 2 inventory until this PI resolves. Future engagement Phase 2 sessions for non-dogfood clients will face the same gap until this PI resolves — that schedules urgency.

Affected: All 14 domains' representation in V2.

description:
PI-074 added the ``executive_summary`` TEXT NULL column to ``planning_items`` (also ``decisions`` and ``sessions``) with a CHECK constraint ``executive_summary IS NULL OR length(executive_summary) BETWEEN 200 AND 800``. PI-075 was scoped to backfill + tighten the constraint to NOT NULL but hasn't been executed. Current state (as of SES-102 authoring): 95 PIs in main's CRMBUILDER.db, 3 with executive_summary populated, 92 needing backfill.

Scope:

(1) Iterate over every PI in the database (``GET /planning-items?include_deleted=true&limit=...`` paginated as needed — current count fits in one page).

(2) For each PI lacking an executive_summary (``executive_summary IS NULL``), read the PI's ``title`` and ``description`` fields.

(3) Generate an executive_summary respecting the CHECK constraint:
    - Length: 200..800 characters inclusive (strict — values outside the range fail the CHECK at PATCH time)
    - Audience: a product manager or executive reviewing the governance log; should communicate the gist of the PI without implementer-facing detail
    - Style: declarative, present tense, no implementation jargon, no internal identifier references (the reader sees the identifier in the PI header)
    - Source material: the PI's own title + description; do not invent context not present in the source

(4) PATCH each PI's executive_summary via the REST API (``PATCH /planning-items/{identifier}`` with ``{"executive_summary": "..."}``). Idempotent: re-running on a PI that already has a summary is a no-op (the script SHOULD detect existing values and skip).

(5) Audit: at completion, query GET /planning-items?include_deleted=true again and confirm every row has an executive_summary populated. Capture the count delta in the session's close-out artifacts.

Implementation candidates:

(a) Manual one-by-one with operator review of each generated summary — high quality, slow. Estimated ~6 hours for 92 PIs at ~4 minutes each.

(b) Scripted bulk generation via an LLM tool call per PI, no operator review — fast but inconsistent quality. ~30 minutes machine time.

(c) Hybrid: scripted bulk generation produces draft summaries; operator reviews and edits a small random sample; corrections feed back as a prompt-tuning iteration; second-pass bulk generation with the refined prompt. ~2 hours.

Recommended: option (c). The 200..800 char constraint provides a hard quality floor; LLM-generated summaries at that length are typically usable but benefit from one review iteration to catch systematic patterns (e.g., over-quoting the description rather than summarizing it).

Deliverable: a session in V2 that records the backfill run, with the PATCH calls itemized in the session's apply prompt and an audit-result artifact (a list of (PI identifier, generated summary length) tuples) committed alongside.

Does not affect ``decisions`` or ``sessions`` executive_summary backfill — those are separate PIs to be authored at their own discretion.

description:
Every close-out's first apply attempt has been failing on the post-PI-073 / DEC-314 ``complete_session_requires_conversation`` validation rule, requiring a manual retry to converge. Three back-to-back occurrences in 24 hours (SES-101 first apply at DEP-088; SES-103 first apply at DEP-091; SES-105 first apply at DEP-094) — each had to be re-run, each successfully created the session on second attempt. The cause: ``_SECTIONS`` in ``crmbuilder-v2/scripts/apply_close_out.py`` orders ``session`` before ``conversation``, but a ``complete`` session requires at least one inbound ``conversation_belongs_to_session`` edge at create-validation time. The conversation that emits that edge is processed in the NEXT section and hasn't been POSTed yet when the session validation fires.

Fix: swap ``_SECTIONS[0]`` and ``_SECTIONS[1]`` so ``conversation`` is processed before ``session``. Update the module docstring's apply-order diagram. Add tests covering single-pass apply (no retry needed).

Safe because: conversation creation already tolerates a not-yet-existent ``conversation_belongs_to_session`` target — the three first-apply failures above ALL successfully created the conversation row plus its inline reference edge; only the session failed. The access layer (``conversations.py``) has an explicit comment at the edge-validation logic: 'there is no complete-requires-session-edge rule' — the membership IS the session linkage and runs from create, not deferred to complete status. Context dictionary (``apply_close_out.py:580-592``) is pre-populated from payload data before the section loop, so no shape-helper depends on POST-response ordering.

Independent of PI-090 (apply-pipeline validator), which will catch broader payload-shape violations at pre-flight; PI-099 fixes the specific ordering bug. Related but distinct: four other inbound-edge requirements in the access layer (``close_out_payloads.py:105`` payload_requires_producing_conversation_edge; ``close_out_payloads.py:150`` applied_payload_requires_successful_deposit_event_edge; ``work_tickets.py:102`` consumed_work_ticket_requires_consumption_edge; ``deposit_events.py:213`` deposit_event_requires_applies_close_out_payload_edge) are structurally identical but currently handled by lazy-create paths or atomic POSTs at apply time — not tripping today. Worth a follow-on PI if any of those start tripping in future workflows.

Meta-validation: this PI is filed AND resolved in the same close-out (SES-107 / CNV-009). The close-out's own apply is the proof — if PI-099 worked, the apply succeeds in one pass; if not, it would fail with the same complete_session_requires_conversation error.
executive_summary: Closes a 24-hour-old recurring bug where every close-out's first apply attempt failed on the new post-PI-073 complete_session_requires_conversation validation rule. The apply script was POSTing the session before the conversation that emits the session's required inbound edge. One-line swap of _SECTIONS[0] and _SECTIONS[1] in apply_close_out.py restores the correct ordering. The fix is safe because conversation creation already tolerates a not-yet-existent target session — three first-apply failures in 24 hours all proved this empirically. Meta-validated by being filed and resolved in the same close-out: if PI-099 works, this very apply lands in one pass.
title: Fix apply_close_out.py section ordering — conversation must POST before session to satisfy complete_session_requires_conversation validation

description: Decide whether SQLite-per-engagement holds at the target peak number of concurrent writers against a single engagement, or whether a different store (e.g. Postgres with row-level MVCC) is required. The engine is SQLite with serialized writers (BEGIN IMMEDIATE, busy_timeout=5000, isolation_level=None, no WAL) and one DB file per engagement, so write concurrency is per-engagement: cross-engagement scales horizontally for free; within one engagement everything funnels through a single serialized writer. Distinguish logical concurrency (many area-partitioned agents, short writes - SQLite survives) from throughput concurrency (sustained rate beyond a single writer, or long-held write transactions - SQLite does not). First input needed: order-of-magnitude peak concurrent writers against one engagement, and whether their work partitions cleanly or contends on shared records. The edit-locking / modify-modify mechanism was split out to PI-103; this item is substrate-adequacy only.
executive_summary: The two concurrency threads deferred from CNV-012. Thread 1, promoted records: choose the modify-modify protection for already-real records once the SessionDraftToken (DEC-325) retires, among an optimistic version/precondition check, an advisory lease, or a hybrid. Thread 2, substrate: validate whether SQLite-per-engagement (serialized writers, BEGIN IMMEDIATE, busy_timeout, no WAL) holds at the target peak concurrent-writer count against one engagement, or whether a store with row-level MVCC such as Postgres is required. Also folds in the CNV-012 companion fix: apply_close_out.py treats HTTP 409 as a benign skip and exits 0, silently dropping genuine collisions, and should hard-fail when an identifier matches but content differs.
title: Validate the storage substrate at target concurrent-writer scale

description: The close-out pre-flight validator (shipped to catch payload problems before apply) let all three of the SES-110 apply's real defects through, emitting only identifier_heads warnings. Two were missing required fields — executive_summary on the decision and on the planning item, now NOT NULL — and one was a wrong-case status value (work_ticket_status "Ready" vs the lowercase enum "ready"). Scope: extend the validator so pre-flight rejects (a) any record missing a required field, including executive_summary on decisions/planning_items/sessions, and (b) any status value not matching its lowercase enum, for every status-bearing entity. These are exactly the class of error the validator exists to prevent; catching them at pre-flight removes the manual-retry churn recent close-outs have each absorbed.
executive_summary: The close-out pre-flight validator let all three of the SES-110 apply's defects through, emitting only identifier-head warnings. Two were missing required fields (executive_summary, now NOT NULL on decisions and planning items) and one was a wrong-case status value (capitalized instead of the lowercase enum). This item hardens the validator to reject missing required fields and incorrect enum casing at pre-flight, which is exactly the class of error it exists to prevent, removing the manual-retry churn that has cost recent close-outs an extra apply pass each.
title: Harden the close-out pre-flight validator to reject missing required fields and wrong-case status values

description: models.py:140 declares decisions.executive_summary as nullable=True, but the live CRMBUILDER database enforces NOT NULL with a 200-800 character CHECK (tightened when executive_summary was made required, PI-075). The SQLAlchemy source no longer describes the actual schema, which misleads anyone reading the model. Scope: reconcile the model definition to the live database for executive_summary on decisions, and audit planning_items and sessions for the same drift, then align all three. Harmless at runtime today (the live DB is authoritative) but a correctness/maintainability defect in the source of record. Decide whether to add an Alembic migration to formalize the constraint or simply correct the model's nullable flag to match.
executive_summary: The SQLAlchemy model declares the decisions.executive_summary column as nullable, but the live governance database enforces NOT NULL with a 200-800 character constraint, so the source no longer matches the schema it describes. This item reconciles the model definitions to the live database for executive_summary across decisions, and checks planning_items and sessions for the same drift, so the code accurately describes the actual schema and future authors are not misled by a model that disagrees with the database.
title: Reconcile the executive_summary column nullability between the SQLAlchemy models and the live database

description: When a draft governance record is promoted to a real record, the per-session draft token that protected it during creation (the SessionDraftToken, decided this session) retires — leaving already-real records with no protection against two agents editing the same record and silently overwriting one another (the modify-modify / lost-update problem). Scope: choose the protection for edits to promoted records — an optimistic version/precondition check that rejects a stale write (reuse updated_at as a compare-and-swap, or add a monotonic row_version; note no row_version exists today), an advisory lease that signals who is editing over the crash-safe floor, or a hybrid. This is the edit-locking half of the concurrency model: distinct from the draft-phase write authorization already decided, and distinct from the storage-substrate adequacy question. Split out of the former combined item so it gets a focused conversation. Opens against the promoted-record-concurrency kickoff (WT-056) alongside the substrate item.
executive_summary: When a draft governance record is promoted to a real record, the per-session draft token that protected it during creation retires, leaving already-real records with no protection against two agents editing the same record and silently overwriting each other. This item chooses the modify-modify protection for promoted records: an optimistic version/precondition check that rejects a stale write, an advisory lease that signals who is editing, or a hybrid. It is the edit-locking half of the concurrency model, distinct from the draft-phase authorization already decided and from the storage-substrate question, and guarantees no lost updates as agent concurrency rises.
title: Edit-locking for already-promoted records: choose the modify-modify protection (optimistic version-check vs advisory lease)

description: The v2 MCP remote-access server (DEC-327) is complete, spec-compliant, and works with compliant MCP clients (Claude Code CLI, Claude Desktop) at https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai. claude.ai-web cannot use it because of an upstream connector regression: it completes the full OAuth 2.1 + PKCE flow and receives a valid token, then never attaches the bearer token to the follow-up MCP request, reporting 'Authorization with the MCP server failed (ofid_*)' (DEC-328; anthropics/claude-ai-mcp #155, anthropics/claude-code #46140; began ~2026-04-15, affects all self-hosted OAuth MCP servers). No server-side fix exists. This item tracks the cross-session pending work: monitor those upstream issues and, once Anthropic ships the bearer-attachment fix, re-test the claude.ai-web custom connector against https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai and confirm it connects and lists the tools. No code change is expected — the server is already ready. Resolves when claude.ai-web connects successfully, or when the goal is formally abandoned in favor of the DEC-245 chat-UI-on-API client (PI-052). Also a good moment to remove the stale 'claude.ai CRMBuilder MCP' synced connector entry that routes through the buggy proxy.

title: Re-test and enable the claude.ai-web custom connector once Anthropic ships the post-OAuth bearer-attachment fix

description:
PI-074 added the executive_summary column to planning_items, decisions, and sessions; PI-075 tightened it to NOT NULL with a 200..800 char CHECK. PI-073 added conversation_executive_summary to conversations (and possibly other prefixed entity tables). The desktop UI surfaces some via PI-091/092/093. The write surfaces — MCP tools, API endpoint schemas, and access-layer patchable-fields lists — are not consistently updated to match.

Empirical evidence from the SES-114 close-out attempt (CNV-016) documents the gap at FIVE distinct layers:

(1) MCP update_planning_item: returned "got an unexpected keyword argument 'executive_summary'" — tool parameter list lacks the field.

(2) MCP create_planning_item: returned HTTP 500 when filing PI-105/106 because executive_summary could not be supplied to a NOT NULL column — tool parameter list lacks the field for create too.

(3) MCP create_conversation: returned HTTP 422 because the API Pydantic schema rejected the call without conversation_executive_summary, and the MCP tool didn't even attempt to pass it.

(4) Curl POST /conversations with conversation_executive_summary in the body: succeeded but SILENTLY DROPPED the field — the response showed conversation_executive_summary: null despite a 600-char value being submitted. The API's POST handler accepts the field in the request schema but does not persist it.

(5) Curl PATCH /conversations/{id} with either conversation_executive_summary or executive_summary: rejected with either 'unknown_field' (the prefixed name hits the access-layer's _PATCHABLE_FIELDS check which apparently has the bare name listed) or 'Extra inputs are not permitted' (the bare name hits the Pydantic UpdateIn schema which only knows the prefixed name). The field is not patchable on conversations through any layer.

Net effect: PI-105's CNV-016 will permanently have conversation_executive_summary=null until this PI lands. The REST API itself is inconsistent across CreateIn / UpdateIn schemas, access-layer _PATCHABLE_FIELDS, and the actual persistence path.

PI-097's pre-condition note documents that DecisionUpdateIn was patched ad-hoc during SES-104's backfill and "may be reverted by the parallel Option A session." Whether that patch survived is one of the audit questions.

Scope:

(1) FULL AUDIT across every write path for every entity carrying executive_summary or its parent-prefixed variant. Entities confirmed to have such a column: planning_items (executive_summary, NOT NULL), decisions (executive_summary, NOT NULL), sessions (session_executive_summary, NOT NULL probably), conversations (conversation_executive_summary, currently allows NULL given CNV-016 was created without it). Audit each entity across: API POST schema (CreateIn), API PATCH schema (UpdateIn/PatchIn), API PUT schema (ReplaceIn if separate), access-layer create function, access-layer update function, access-layer _PATCHABLE_FIELDS, and MCP tool parameter lists for create_* and update_*.

(2) The audit should produce a matrix: for each entity × each layer, does the field exist, accept the right name (bare vs prefixed), persist correctly, and validate the 200..800 length range?

(3) Patch the API where the schema rejects valid input or where the persistence path silently drops a accepted field.

(4) Patch the access layer where _PATCHABLE_FIELDS is missing entries or uses the wrong name.

(5) Patch the MCP tools where create_* or update_* parameter lists lack the field.

(6) Add test coverage at every layer (API, access, MCP) for happy path AND length-validation failure on create AND update for every entity touched.

(7) Smoke-verify end-to-end: refresh PI-031's stale executive_summary via MCP; refresh CNV-016's null conversation_executive_summary via MCP; close out a new session entirely through MCP rather than curl.

(8) Investigate whether the parent-prefix field-naming convention has been applied consistently across the API surface. Possible follow-on: the convention drift between MCP (bare names), API POST (prefixed names), API PATCH (mixed?), and access layer (mixed?) may itself need a consolidating refactor or at minimum a documented mapping layer.

Out of scope:
- Desktop UI changes (owned by PI-091/092/093)
- apply_close_out.py changes (already handles executive_summary in payloads)
- A systemic column-vs-write-surface audit beyond executive_summary — file as a follow-on if the audit work surfaces evidence other fields drift similarly

Acceptance: every entity × write-path × field-name combination is validated by tests; every MCP create/update tool accepts and persists executive_summary correctly; every REST endpoint accepts both POST and PATCH of the field; CNV-016 gets its conversation_executive_summary populated through the same tool surface a future close-out would use.</parameter>
</invoke>
executive_summary: MCP create_planning_item and update_planning_item do not accept executive_summary as a parameter, and PI-097 notes the parallel gap on the API PatchIn schemas was patched ad-hoc and may have been reverted. PI-074 made the column NOT NULL, so the gap blocks both refreshing stale summaries and filing new PIs via MCP. This PI audits all three entity types and patches both layers.
title: Expose executive_summary on MCP create/update tools for planning_items, decisions, and sessions (and verify API schemas)

description:
Show each planning item's creation datetime and last-edited datetime in the V2 desktop Planning Items panel so operators can tell when a PI was filed and last changed.

## Current state (investigated 2026-05-29)

- **Model:** `crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/models.py` — `PlanningItem` already defines `created_at` (`default=_utcnow`) and `updated_at` (`default=_utcnow, onupdate=_utcnow`), both `DateTime(timezone=True)`, NOT NULL.
- **API:** `GET /planning-items` and `GET /planning-items/{id}` already return `created_at` and `updated_at` (confirmed live against PI-076; the access layer's `to_dict` emits them).
- **UI gap:** `crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/panels/planning_items.py` `PlanningItemsPanel` does NOT surface them. `list_columns()` returns identifier/title/type/status only; `render_detail()` shows identifier/type/status/resolution_reference/executive_summary/description/references and no timestamps.

**Therefore no migration and no API change are needed.** This is a UI-only task. Do not add new columns.

## Scope

1. In `PlanningItemsPanel.render_detail()`, add a metadata section (or form rows) showing `Created` and `Last Updated`, formatted for human reading (local time, e.g. `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM`). Handle missing/null defensively.
2. Optionally add a `Created` list column (`ColumnSpec(field="created_at", ...)`) — consider a date-only render and a sensible width.
3. Match the v0.6 styling pass conventions used elsewhere in the panel (label/value form rows, separators, dim styling for secondary metadata).
4. If a shared datetime-formatting helper does not already exist in the UI layer, add a small one rather than inlining `strftime` per call site; check whether other panels (sessions, decisions) need the same display so the helper is reusable.

## Acceptance

- Opening a planning item in the desktop app shows its created and last-updated datetimes.
- The values match what the API returns for that PI.
- No schema migration is introduced; `alembic heads` is unchanged.

## Notes

Sibling timestamp-bearing entities (sessions: `session_created_at`/`session_updated_at`; decisions: `created_at`/`updated_at`; conversations, workstreams, etc.) have the same UI-surfacing gap. Generalizing the display helper across panels could be a natural follow-on, but is out of scope for PI-107 unless trivially free.
executive_summary: The planning_items table already carries created_at and updated_at columns (access/models.py PlanningItem, default=_utcnow / onupdate=_utcnow) and the REST API already returns both keys in GET /planning-items responses (verified live). The only gap is the desktop UI: the Planning Items panel neither lists nor displays either timestamp. This item surfaces the existing values — add a Created (and optionally Updated) field to the detail render in PlanningItemsPanel and a created-date list column — so an operator can see when a planning item was filed and last edited without querying the API. No schema migration or API change is required; the work is UI-only and should format the timestamps for display (local time, human-readable) consistent with the v0.6 styling pass.
title: Surface planning_item created/updated timestamps in the Planning Items UI panel

description: Make created/last-edited timestamp visibility universal across the V2 desktop UI, realizing REQ-002 and extending PI-107 (commit f690bde, Planning Items panel) to every first-class entity panel. Scope: first-class UI entities only (governance + methodology entities with a browsable panel); subsidiary catalog/edge/log tables excluded. Each in-scope panel gains a formatted Created list column and Created / Last Updated detail rows via the existing ui/widgets/datetime_format.format_timestamp helper; Sessions/Workstreams/Conversations have their raw unformatted updated_at replaced with the formatted value. Immutable/append-only types (deposit_event, reference, topic, charter, status, reference_book_version) render created + an em-dash last-edited and are not given an updated_at column. Storage is already ~95% complete (24/24 carry created_at, 23 carry updated_at); add an Alembic migration only if a browsable type is found lacking a column. Per-panel tests mirror test_planning_items_timestamps.py. Anchored by kickoff WT-057. May be sliced into multiple sessions/commits; the final delivering session resolves this PI.

title: Surface created/last-edited timestamps for all first-class V2 object types in the UI

description:
**Problem.** The slice-A pattern flips a planning_item Open→Resolved atomically when a `resolves` reference is POSTed (server-side side-effect on POST /references). Work_ticket consumption was supposed to follow the same shape: when a session or conversation opens-against a work_ticket, that act IS the consumption — the ticket should transition ready→consumed automatically as the side-effect of the opens-against edge landing. The SES-082 apply prompt for the persona build explicitly claimed this: 'WT-049 transitions ready → consumed via the conversation_opens_against_work_ticket edge processing.' The claim was aspirational; no such side-effect was ever implemented. The persona, field, requirement, manual_config, test_spec, and process-v2 build sessions (SES-082..086) all shipped code and Resolved their PIs cleanly, but every one of them left its kickoff WT stuck at ready. The drift surfaced when WT-049 was investigated four days later and the deposit_event log for SES-082 (dep_064.log) was found to contain no PATCH on /work-tickets/WT-049. For WT-048 (SES-087) the operator caught the gap and issued an explicit PATCH; the explicit-PATCH discipline is fragile by design — it relies on the apply prompt's verification step being run AND acted on.

**Scope of the structural fix.** Add a side-effect to POST /references (or to apply_close_out.py's references shaper, whichever is the cleaner extension point) that: when an inserted reference has `relationship_kind` in {`session_opens_against_work_ticket`, `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`} AND the target work_ticket is currently at status=ready, atomically transition to status=consumed with consumed_at=now(). The existing `_validate_edges` consumed-requires-edge rule already enforces the precondition; the side-effect simply runs the transition that the rule was set up to permit. Single-use violation continues to take precedence — if the work_ticket already has a consumption edge of either kind, the new POST should still 422 with `work_ticket_single_use_violation` rather than silently re-flipping.

**Decision points.** Where the side-effect lives: (a) in the references repository on upsert — purest semantics but couples two unrelated entities; (b) in the apply_close_out.py pipeline after the references section completes — easier to scope and audit but doesn't fire for references created outside the apply pipeline; (c) as an event-handler-style hook the references repo emits and the work_tickets repo consumes. Option (a) parallels the slice-A `resolves` → planning_item flip pattern most directly. Whichever site is chosen, document with a regression test: POST a session_opens_against_work_ticket edge to a ready work_ticket and assert status=consumed without an explicit PATCH.

**Backfill scope.** Commit 2c9759d retired WT-049..054. WT-056, WT-057 are currently ready and unrelated to this drift (they're future-anticipated tickets from the current orchestrator workstream, not stale). No other backfill needed.
executive_summary: apply_close_out.py treats `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` and `session_opens_against_work_ticket` references as ordinary edges with no side-effect. Six SES-082..086 apply prompts assumed those edges would auto-flip the target work_ticket from ready to consumed (mirroring slice-A's `resolves` → planning_item flip), and the assumption silently left WT-049..054 stuck at ready for four days until commit 2c9759d caught up the data. Implement the auto-transition as a structural fix so the next cohort can't drift the same way.
title: Auto-transition work_tickets on inbound opens-against edge in apply pipeline
